nativeRange.habitat: Rivers, streams, lakes, cenotes, and karst limestone spring systems across Cuba; tolerates hard, alkaline, and often oxygen-poor waters typical of Cuban karst geology

decorPreferences: large flat rocks and caves for territory establishment and spawning sites
heavy substrate (coarse gravel or sand) tolerant of vigorous digging
robust or artificial plants only — will uproot and destroy live plants
unobstructed open swimming area in front half of tank

diet.notes: Predatory carnivore in the wild, feeding on fish, invertebrates, worms, and crustaceans. In captivity accepts high-quality large cichlid pellets, frozen/thawed whole shrimp, earthworms, mussel, and raw fish flesh. Live feeder fish are unnecessary and risk disease introduction. Avoid fatty mammalian meats (beef heart) as a staple. Feed 1–2 times daily in moderate amounts; a structured diet supporting coloration is advisable.

goodWith: robust large cichlids of similar size (with extreme caution and large tank)
large armoured catfish such as Plecostomus species
established bonded conspecific pair in a sufficiently large species-only tank
avoidWith: smaller fish of any species (will be eaten or killed)
similarly aggressive cichlids unless tank is very large with clear territories
multiple males without established pair bond

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Nandopsis tetracanthus; verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: The Cuban Cichlid is a large, powerful Caribbean Heroini endemic to Cuba, reaching 25 cm. Adapted to hard karst limestone waters, it is a highly aggressive substrate-spawner that forms strong pair bonds with biparental fry defence. Rarely seen in the hobby; suitable only for experienced large-cichlid specialists.
careNotes:
Nandopsis tetracanthus is the sole cichlid species endemic to Cuba and one of the most territorially aggressive large cichlids available in the hobby. A minimum tank of 400 L (footprint 150 cm or longer) is recommended for a single specimen or a proven bonded pair; considerably more space is needed if any tankmates are attempted. The species is native to Cuba’s karst limestone river systems and spring-fed cenotes, giving it a natural tolerance for hard, alkaline, and occasionally calcium-rich water — pH 7.0–8.5 and moderate-to-hard general hardness are acceptable, making it unusually adaptable to harder tap water supplies. Soft or acidic water should be avoided. Temperature range of 22–28°C is appropriate; the upper end supports spawning activity.

This cichlid is an exceptionally destructive tank occupant: it will excavate substrate vigorously, uproot all plants, and rearrange rockwork. All decor should be stable and heavy. Live plants are impractical; use robust artificial alternatives or forego planting entirely. Filtration must be powerful and efficient — the species produces significant waste and is intolerant of degraded water quality despite its hardness tolerance. Weekly large water changes (30–50%) are strongly recommended.

The Cuban Cichlid should not be mixed with smaller fish under any circumstances; even fish of considerable size may be attacked or killed. Keeping a single specimen is the safest approach; a bonded pair can coexist but requires careful introduction with close monitoring. Any tankmates must be of similar or greater size and robustness, and the aquarist should be prepared to separate individuals quickly. The species is rarely available in the hobby trade and is occasionally imported through specialist cichlid dealers. See also: Caribbean Heroini cichlids (Nandopsis haitiensis, Archocentrus species).
breedingNotes: Nandopsis tetracanthus is a substrate spawner that forms a strong, stable pair bond. Spawning is triggered by stable warm water (25–28°C), good feeding condition, and the presence of a large flat rock or cave as a spawning site. The female deposits up to several hundred adhesive eggs on a cleaned flat surface; both parents share incubation duties, fanning and guarding the clutch aggressively. Eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days at 26°C, and the wriggling larvae are typically moved into pre-dug pits in the substrate by the parents. Free-swimming fry emerge after a further 3–5 days and can be fed baby brine shrimp, microworms, and finely crushed cichlid fry food. Parental defence is intense — both parents will attack the aquarist’s hand and any tank occupants without hesitation during the brooding period. Remove any non-pair tankmates before spawning commences. Pair compatibility can be difficult to achieve; introducing two juveniles and allowing a natural pair bond to form is the recommended approach rather than forcing an adult pairing.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nandopsis tetracanthus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, native range (Cuba endemic), water parameters (pH, hardness, temperature), habitat description (karst limestone springs and rivers), diet (carnivore), aggression level, tank requirements, and breeding behaviour notes including substrate-spawning and biparental care.

additional.name: FishBase — Nandopsis tetracanthus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 25 cm TL), native range (Cuba), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet (carnivore/invertivore), habitat (freshwater rivers and streams), and reproduction mode (substrate spawner with parental care).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cichlid Care Guides
additional.notes: General large New World cichlid husbandry guidance used to cross-check tank size recommendations, filtration requirements, diet, and water change frequency for large carnivorous cichlids. No dedicated N. tetracanthus page; applied comparative guidance from large cichlid category.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Cuban Cichlid (Nandopsis tetracanthus)
additional.notes: Species profile confirming Cuba-endemic status, aggressive temperament, biparental substrate-spawning behaviour, hard alkaline water tolerance from karst limestone springs, recommended minimum tank size for a pair, and rarity in the UK/European hobby trade.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum.com — Nandopsis tetracanthus species profile
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid community species profile; supplementary data on conspecific aggression, pair bonding dynamics, tank decor requirements (robust rockwork, no live plants), breeding triggers, and fry care. Used to cross-check breedingDifficulty and compatibility assessments.

fish.breedingNotes: Substrate spawner; bonded pair clean and defend a flat rock or cave site. Both parents guard eggs and free-swimming fry with intense aggression. Achieving a compatible pair bond is the primary challenge — best done by raising juveniles together. Brood sizes up to several hundred; fry raised on BBS and finely crushed foods.
